text: The robotic surgical procedure begins with the reduction of the hiatal hernia contents, which include thirty percent of the proximal stomach.

The surgeon uses four robotic arms to achieve tension and counter-traction, entering the lesser sac at the level of the cardia. This approach facilitates a tension-free Nissen fundoplication and improves visualization of the left crura.
The surgeon carefully preserves the peritoneum overlying the crura to optimize the repair of the hernia defect during the cruroplasty.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament on the right side to enhance visualization of the right crura.
The surgeon carefully dissects to mobilize and free the attachments of the hernia sac from the posterior mediastinal structures, facilitating the reduction of the hernia contents.
The surgeon navigates towards the junction of the right and left crura, with the inferior vena cava visible to the left of the right crus in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the left crux and maneuvers behind the esophagus, visualizing the instrument at the angle of His. The esophagus is encircled to facilitate manipulation of the gastroesophageal junction, continuing dissection within the mediastinum to mobilize and reduce the hernia sac and its contents.
The surgeon encounters a prominent hernia sac associated with larger anal hernias. To minimize the risk of incorporating the hernia sac during the fundoplication, the hernia sac is carefully removed.
The surgeon removes the hernia sac, enhancing the visualization of the gastroesophageal junction, which is crucial for the subsequent steps of the procedure.

The surgeon initiates a posterior cruroplasty using a zero-ethyl bond permanent suture in a figure-of-eight pattern. The robotic platform, with its seven degrees of freedom, facilitates precise manipulation of the instruments, suture, and needle for accurate placement.
The surgeon performs an anterior cruroplasty, with a fully French bougie positioned in the esophagus and stomach for calibration, ensuring precise alignment during the procedure.

The surgeon introduces a biological mesh to support the anterior and posterior cruroplasty. The mesh is positioned around the esophagus but does not encircle it completely, minimizing the risk of esophageal stretching. The mesh is secured with interrupted sutures, avoiding the use of tacks to prevent potential injury to structures beneath the diaphragm.

The surgeon initiates the fundoplication by placing multiple gastro-gastric stitches over a two-centimeter distance to create the wrap. The earlier mobilization and treatment of the cardia ensure a tension-free fundoplication.

The completed surgical field is shown, with the gastroesophageal junction clearly visible following the removal of the hernia sac and the completion of anterior and posterior cruroplasty using biological mesh and sutures.
